---
title: "B-mode systematics in<br>the UNIONS-3500 weak lensing survey"
occasion: CNRS AI Rising Talents — Interview
description: "Deep-dive: B-mode analysis, systematics characterisation, cosmological constraints"
author:
    name: Cail Daley
    affiliation: "CosmoStat, CEA Paris-Saclay  <br> <br> [on behalf of the UNIONS weak lensing team]{style=font-size:1.3em}"
date: June 25, 2026
date-format: long

format:
    revealjs:
        width:  1920
        height: 1080
        margin: 0
        auto-stretch: false
        theme: [default, custom.scss]
        slide-number: true
        fig-align: center
        menu: false
        include-after-body: assets/no_footer_on_titleslide.html
        footer: "Cail Daley | CNRS AI Rising Talents | June 25, 2026"
        history: false
        hash: true
        pdf-separate-fragments: true
        pdf-max-pages-per-slide: 1
        template-partials:
            - title-slide.html
        center-title-slide: false
        logo: images/unions_wl_logo.png

html-math-method:
    method: mathjax
    url: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg-full.js
include-in-header: assets/mathjax.html
---

## UNIONS is the first deep weak-lensing map of the northern sky {#unions-opportunity}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:55%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em"}
- MegaCam/CFHT, **~3,500 deg²** of deep $r$-band imaging; **~61 million source galaxies**,
  $n_\mathrm{eff} \approx 5 \, \mathrm{arcmin}^{-2}$
- The only wide, deep survey of its generation covering the **northern sky** — minimal overlap
  with KiDS, DES, HSC, so an **independent** cosmic-shear measurement
- Statistical power of a next-generation survey's **first year**, and a footprint that pairs
  naturally with **Euclid**
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em;font-style:italic"}
And produced by a team an **order of magnitude smaller** than DES or KiDS.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:45%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/des_y6_footprints_unions.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
~1.5 min. Open broad and exciting: UNIONS earns its place in the S₈/systematics landscape on
its own terms — unique northern footprint (independent of the southern surveys), next-gen
first-year statistical power, the natural Euclid pairing. The new beat: the team is an order of
magnitude smaller — that the science below came from a small team is part of what makes it
impressive. (Keep this as *context*, not a program pitch — the program lives in Talk 1.)
:::


## Weak lensing reads the cosmic web from the shapes of galaxies {#shear-intro}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:56%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em"}
Mass between us and a distant galaxy bends its light, **shearing its observed shape** by a
percent or two. Averaged over millions of galaxies, that tiny coherent distortion maps the
**large-scale matter distribution** — the cosmic web.
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.7em"}
We measure it through the **two-point shear correlation** — how the shapes of galaxy pairs
align as a function of separation — the statistical signal of the matter between.
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.6em;font-style:italic"}
The catch: the signal is buried in the galaxies' own *intrinsic* shapes and in every
imperfection of the measurement. Getting the shapes right is the whole game.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:44%"}

::: {style="border:2px dashed #BC4538;border-radius:0.4em;padding:2em 1em;text-align:center;color:#888;font-style:italic;margin-top:1.0em"}
[ shear / shape-measurement<br>pipeline diagram to come ]<br><br>
sky → images → PSF → galaxy shapes → shear → correlations
:::

<!-- IMAGE PLACEHOLDER (slide-images constitution): a clean flowchart of the full shear
     shape-measurement process — Cail's idea. sky → imaging → PSF modelling → shape
     measurement → calibration → two-point correlations. Conveys "this is a long, careful
     pipeline" and sets up why systematics validation matters. -->

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
~1.5 min. The "what is weak lensing" slide — pitched for a general jury. Lensing shears shapes;
averaged over millions of galaxies it maps the matter; we read it through the two-point shear
correlation. End on the difficulty: the cosmological signal hides under intrinsic shapes and
measurement systematics, so the shape catalog is everything — which sets up the whole talk. The
flowchart (placeholder) shows the long careful pipeline; image-gen fiber will make it.
:::


## Cosmic shear makes only E-modes — so B-modes flag systematics {#shear-eb}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:60%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em"}
Gravitational lensing leaves a specific geometric fingerprint. To leading order the shear field
is **curl-free**, and decomposes into:
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em"}
- **E-modes** (gradient-like): the cosmological lensing signal
- **B-modes** (curl-like): sourced only by higher-order effects far below current sensitivity —
  so **effectively zero from gravity alone**
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em;font-style:italic"}
At Stage-III sensitivity, a detected **B-mode is a clean flag for residual systematics** — PSF
leakage, additive shear bias, calibration error. (The converse doesn't hold: no B-modes does
not prove no systematics.)
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:40%"}

:::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![E-mode pattern](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/emode_pattern.jpg){width=100%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![B-mode pattern](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/bmode_pattern.jpg){width=100%}
:::
::::::

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
~1.5 min. The key concept, science corrected to the paper: scalar lensing is curl-free to
leading order → gradient-like E-modes (signal), curl-like B-modes (sourced only by tiny
higher-order effects, so ≈0). At current sensitivity a detected B-mode means a systematic. State
the honest caveat — absence of B-modes doesn't rule out contamination. The pattern images are
intuitive. This is the "what could go wrong, and how we'd see it" slide.
:::


## Before any cosmology, the shear catalog must be clean enough {#first-step}

::: {style="margin-top:1.2em;font-size:1.05em"}
A single null test only sees the scales it is built for. So the question is sharper than "are
there B-modes?" — it is **"clean across *which* scales, in *which* basis?"**
:::

::: {style="margin-top:1.1em"}
> The first UNIONS release is the existence proof that a small team can characterise these
> systematics, end to end, before trusting a single cosmological number.
:::

::: {style="margin-top:1.1em;font-style:italic"}
That is the work in this talk: turning a B-mode null test into a tool that *decides* the
analysis.
:::

::: notes
~45 sec. The hinge: zoom from "B-modes flag systematics" to the real problem — any single test
is scale-limited and basis-dependent, so a clean answer needs multiple tests across scales.
Frame the B-mode work as the existence proof for end-to-end systematics control by a small team.
Sets up the three statistics.
:::


## I use three E/B-separable statistics, because each has different blind spots {#three-stats}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:54%"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.3em;font-size:0.95em"}
1. **$\xi_\pm^B(\theta)$** — pure-mode correlation functions; a direct real-space E/B split
2. **COSEBIs** — discrete orthogonal modes, maximally sensitive to B-modes on specific scales
3. **$C_\ell^{BB}$** — catalog-based harmonic-space power spectra; a different scale weighting
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em"}
They probe the *same* shear field, but their filter functions **weight angular scales
differently**, and the mapping between bases is not one-to-one.
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em;font-style:italic"}
So contamination that one basis absorbs, another exposes. The full data vector is rich — the
point is what happens **when the three disagree.**
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:46%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/pure_eb_data_vector.png){width=92%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
~1.5 min. Three complementary bases; each weights scales differently, so comparing across them
exposes systematics any single one would absorb (the paper's central methodological point).
Keep the data vector HIGH-LEVEL here — it's the figure on this slide, not its own slide (per
Cail). Don't walk every panel; the message is "three bases, and they need not agree." Sets up
the disagreement.
:::


## The statistics disagree — and that disagreement is the information {#disagreement}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:52%"}

Over the **full angular range**:

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em"}
- $\xi_\pm^B$: **passes** ✓
- $C_\ell^{BB}$: **passes** ✓
- **COSEBIs: fails** ✗
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
COSEBIs concentrate sensitivity exactly where the contamination lives. The signature is an
**oscillating B-mode pattern at the CCD scale**.
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.45em;font-size:0.9em;font-style:italic"}
A detector imprints a near-constant ellipticity on each CCD; the jumps at chip boundaries carry
curl, generating B-modes at the CCD scale. Same MegaCam camera as **CFHTLenS**, where it was
first reported (Asgari et al. 2019).
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:48%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/cosebis_bsigma_talk.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
~2 min, the key finding. COSEBIs are built to be maximally sensitive to specific B-mode
patterns, so when they fail and the others pass, we have *localised* the contamination. The
mechanism is concrete and from the paper: constant ellipticity per CCD, discontinuities at chip
boundaries carry curl → B-modes at CCD angular scales; a known MegaCam additive-shear-bias
effect first seen in CFHTLenS. The intellectual move: disagreement is the analysis working as
designed.
:::


## The disagreement selects the catalog and sets the scale cuts {#scale-cuts}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:50%"}

The joint requirement — pass **all three** statistics — does real work:

**1. Catalog selection**

B-mode failures on early catalogs drove tighter **galaxy size cuts** and **stellar-halo
masking**; the size-cut catalog is adopted as fiducial.

**2. Angular scale cuts**

The cuts that suppress the CCD-scale signal become the **scale cuts for the cosmology papers** —
locked, within a **blinded** framework, before any S₈ is revealed.

:::
::: {.column style="width:50%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/config_space_pte_fiducial.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
~1.5 min. The disagreement does work: it selects the catalog version (size cut + halo masking,
per the paper — the size-cut catalog is fiducial) and sets the scale cuts. Both locked before
unblinding — principled blinding discipline, immune to fishing for a good S₈. PTE-grid figure
shows the stable acceptable region the adopted cuts sit in.
:::


## Our S₈ is conservative by design — wider error bars, on purpose {#cosmology}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:55%;font-size:0.94em"}

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em"}
With a validated catalog and frozen scale cuts, the two independent analysis conventions find:

- **S₈ = 0.86 ± 0.08** (configuration space) and **S₈ = 0.92 ± 0.08** (harmonic space)
- Consistent with each other, and with the early-universe value ($0.834 \pm 0.016$) to ~1σ
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.5em;font-size:0.95em"}
The error bars are **wider** than the recent KiDS and DES releases — deliberately. A small team
made conservative choices and inflated its systematic budget throughout.
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.4em;font-size:0.92em;font-style:italic"}
We avoid the failure mode the history of S₈ has now diagnosed: a tight constraint that moves
once systematics are better understood.
:::

:::
::: {.column style="width:45%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/contour_survey_comparison.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: notes
~1.5 min. Lead with the headline numbers and the agreement between the two conventions, then the
deliberate conservatism: wider bars on purpose, because a small team should inflate its budget
rather than claim a tight constraint that later moves. This is the cross-talk bridge — the same
S₈ cautionary tale that opens Talk 1 closes here.
:::


## Where UNIONS goes next: tomographic, then the multi-probe gold standard {#forward}

:::::::::::::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:55%;font-size:0.9em"}

**This first release delivers**

- The first northern-sky cosmic-shear constraints
- A systematics framework where **disagreement becomes diagnosis**
- An S₈ consistent with the concordance model, conservatively

**The science from here**

- **Tomographic** cosmic shear — the field standard
- **Multi-probe** — shear + galaxy–galaxy lensing + clustering: percent-level S₈ and dark energy
- Cross-correlations into **DESI** and **Planck**

:::
::: {.column style="width:45%"}

![](../26_Moriond_UNIONS/images/s8_convergence_with_unions.png){width=100%}

:::
::::::::::::::

::: {style="margin-top:0.2em;font-style:italic;text-align:center"}
A clean first step, characterised end to end — and produced almost entirely by AI agents under
my direction. Thank you.
:::

::: notes
~1 min. Zoom back to the science future (NOT a program pitch — that lives in Talk 1): tomographic
then multi-probe, percent-level S₈ + dark energy, cross-correlations into DESI/Planck. Use the
convergence figure to show UNIONS joining the S₈ landscape, closing the loop on the northern-sky
opportunity that opened the talk. The agentic line is ONE methodological beat at the very end —
a flourish, not a proposal.
:::
